---
title: "Lesson 2:  Computational Simplifications for the CRLB"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_computational-simplifications-for-the-crlb.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_computational-simplifications-for-the-crlb.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-computational-simplifications-for-the-crlb_module3_lesson2.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## Slides on Computational Simplifications for CRLB {#sec-crlb-computational-simplifications}

Let $\mathbf{X}=(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$ have joint density or probability mass function $f(\mathbf{x};\theta)$.

::: {#def-fisher-information-computational}
## Fisher Information

The Fisher information in the sample $\mathbf{X}$ is

$$
I_n(\theta)
:=
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right)^2
\right]
$$
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Regularity Conditions

The following simplifications assume that the support of $f(\mathbf{x};\theta)$ does not depend on $\theta$ and that differentiation may be passed through the integral or sum
:::

### The Score Has Mean Zero {#sec-score-mean-zero}

::: {#prp-score-mean-zero}
## Mean of the Score

Under the regularity conditions,

$$
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
=0
$$
:::

The result follows from

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
&=
\int
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\right)
 f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&=
\int
\underbrace{
\frac{
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
}{f(\mathbf{x};\theta)}
}_{\displaystyle
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
}
 f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&=
\int
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&=
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\int f(\mathbf{x};\theta)\,d\mathbf{x}
&& \text{differentiate under the integral} \\
&=
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}1 \\
&=0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Fisher Information from the Second Derivative {#sec-fisher-information-second-derivative}

::: {#prp-fisher-information-second-derivative}
## Alternative Fisher Information Formula

Under the regularity conditions,

$$
I_n(\theta)
=
-\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
$$
:::

From @prp-score-mean-zero,

$$
\int
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\right)
 f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x}
=0
$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0
&=
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\int
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\right)
 f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&=
\int
\left(
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\right)
 f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&\qquad+
\int
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\right)
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x}
&& \text{product rule} \\
&=
\int
\left(
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\right)
 f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x} \\
&\qquad+
\int
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\right)^2
 f(\mathbf{x};\theta)
\,d\mathbf{x}
&& \text{since }\frac{\partial f}{\partial\theta}=f\frac{\partial\ln f}{\partial\theta} \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
+
\underbrace{
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right)^2
\right]
}_{\displaystyle I_n(\theta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
I_n(\theta)
=
-\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\theta^2}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right]
$$

### Fisher Information in an IID Sample {#sec-fisher-information-iid-sample}

::: {#prp-fisher-information-iid}
## Additivity of Fisher Information

If $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n$ are independent and identically distributed, then

$$
I_n(\theta)=nI_1(\theta)
$$
:::

For an independent and identically distributed sample,

$$
f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
=
\prod_{i=1}^{n}f(X_i;\theta)
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_n(\theta)
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(\mathbf{X};\theta)
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln \prod_{i=1}^{n}f(X_i;\theta)
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln f(X_i;\theta)
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(X_i;\theta)
\right)
\left(
\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(X_j;\theta)
\right)
\right] \\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(X_i;\theta)
\right)
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(X_j;\theta)
\right)
\right] \\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(X_i;\theta)
\right)^2
\right] \\
&\qquad+
\underbrace{
\sum_{i\ne j}
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(X_i;\theta)
\right]
\mathbb{E}_{\theta}
\left[
\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}
\ln f(X_j;\theta)
\right]
}_{\displaystyle 0\text{ by independence and the mean-zero score property}} \\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}I_1(\theta) \\
&=
nI_1(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

## Exponential Rate Example {#sec-crlb-exponential-rate}

::: {#exm-crlb-exponential-rate}
## Estimating the Exponential Rate

Let

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Exp}(\text{rate}=\lambda)
$$

Find the Cramér--Rao lower bound for the variance of every unbiased estimator $T$ of $\lambda$
:::

For

$$
\tau(\lambda)=\lambda
$$

we have

$$
\mathrm{Var}_{\lambda}(T)
\ge
\frac{[\tau'(\lambda)]^2}{I_n(\lambda)}
$$

The density of one observation is

$$
f(x;\lambda)
=
\lambda e^{-\lambda x}
\mathbb{I}_{(0,\infty)}(x)
$$

The joint density is

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x};\lambda)
&=
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\lambda e^{-\lambda x_i}
\mathbb{I}_{(0,\infty)}(x_i) \\
&=
\lambda^n
\exp\!\left\{-\lambda\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i\right\}
\prod_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{I}_{(0,\infty)}(x_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Because the support is independent of $\lambda$, the log-likelihood on the support is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(\lambda;\mathbf{x})
&=
\ln f(\mathbf{x};\lambda) \\
&=
n\ln\lambda
-
\lambda\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Using the Score {#sec-exponential-fisher-information-score}

Differentiate the log-likelihood:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial\lambda}
\ell(\lambda;\mathbf{x})
&=
\frac{n}{\lambda}
-
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Let

$$
Y=\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
$$

Then

$$
Y\sim\operatorname{Gamma}(\text{shape}=n,\text{rate}=\lambda)
$$

and

$$
\mathbb{E}_{\lambda}[Y]=\frac{n}{\lambda}
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}_{\lambda}(Y)=\frac{n}{\lambda^2}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_n(\lambda)
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\lambda}
\left[
\left(
\frac{\partial}{\partial\lambda}
\ell(\lambda;\mathbf{X})
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\lambda}
\left[
\left(
\frac{n}{\lambda}-Y
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}_{\lambda}
\left[
\left(
Y-\mathbb{E}_{\lambda}[Y]
\right)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathrm{Var}_{\lambda}(Y) \\
&=
\frac{n}{\lambda^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Cramér--Rao lower bound is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Var}_{\lambda}(T)
&\ge
\frac{[\tau'(\lambda)]^2}{I_n(\lambda)} \\
&=
\frac{1^2}{n/\lambda^2} \\
&=
\boxed{\frac{\lambda^2}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Using the Second Derivative {#sec-exponential-fisher-information-second-derivative}

Alternatively,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial\lambda}
\ell(\lambda;\mathbf{x})
&=
\frac{n}{\lambda}
-
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i \\
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\lambda^2}
\ell(\lambda;\mathbf{x})
&=
-\frac{n}{\lambda^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_n(\lambda)
&=
-\mathbb{E}_{\lambda}
\left[
\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\lambda^2}
\ell(\lambda;\mathbf{X})
\right] \\
&=
-\mathbb{E}_{\lambda}
\left[
-\frac{n}{\lambda^2}
\right] \\
&=
\frac{n}{\lambda^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The same calculation could instead be performed for a single observation:

$$
I_1(\lambda)=\frac{1}{\lambda^2}
$$

Then @prp-fisher-information-iid gives

$$
I_n(\lambda)=nI_1(\lambda)=\frac{n}{\lambda^2}
$$

## Exponential Transformation Example {#sec-crlb-exponential-transformation}

::: {#exm-crlb-exponential-transformation}
## Estimating $e^{-\lambda}$

Let

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Exp}(\text{rate}=\lambda)
$$

Find the Cramér--Rao lower bound for the variance of every unbiased estimator $T$ of $e^{-\lambda}$
:::

The Fisher information has already been calculated:

$$
I_n(\lambda)=\frac{n}{\lambda^2}
$$

For

$$
\tau(\lambda)=e^{-\lambda}
$$

we have

$$
\tau'(\lambda)=-e^{-\lambda}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Var}_{\lambda}(T)
&\ge
\frac{[\tau'(\lambda)]^2}{I_n(\lambda)} \\
&=
\frac{[-e^{-\lambda}]^2}{n/\lambda^2} \\
&=
\boxed{\frac{\lambda^2e^{-2\lambda}}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

::: {.callout-warning}
## Correction to the Final Slide

The final slide includes an extra factor of $\lambda$ in $\tau'(\lambda)$. Since $\frac{d}{d\lambda}e^{-\lambda}=-e^{-\lambda}$, the corrected bound is $\lambda^2e^{-2\lambda}/n$
:::


## Reflection
